method3.generated_article:
==Name==
The name “Brecqhou” refers specifically to the island of Brecqhou, whereas “Sark” may refer both to the island of Sark itself and to the wider jurisdiction that includes Brecqhou and other islets and rocks.[1] Historically, Brecqhou was also known as the Isle des Marchants after Jeanne de Chesney married Denis Le Marchant of Guernsey and ownership passed through the Le Marchant family.[2] The name Isle des Marchants continued to be used even after ownership of the island passed to others.[2] An alternative spelling, “Brechou”, appeared on carriage labels issued for the island, where it was used as another form of the island’s name.[5]

==Geography==
Brecqhou is a small island in the Channel Islands, which lie in the English Channel off the coast of Normandy in north-western France.[2][5] It is within the Bailiwick of Guernsey, whose smaller islands include Alderney, Sark, Herm, Jethou, Brecqhou and Lihou.[5] Within the bailiwick, Brecqhou is associated with the Sark jurisdiction, one of three jurisdictions alongside Guernsey and Alderney.[4] The Sark jurisdiction includes Sark and Brecqhou, and also extends to nearby islets and rocks.[3][4] Brecqhou is privately owned and has a very small population compared with Sark, while its separation from Sark by water has contributed to perceptions of distinctness between the two islands.[3]

==Flag and stamps==
No official flag for Brecqhou is identified in the available flag-related material, which contains only fragmentary references to the island and general vexillological topics rather than a described design or formal status.[1][2] Published material on the island instead records heraldic imagery, including the armorials of Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and the Brecqhou shield, as prominent visual symbols associated with the island.[3][4]

Brecqhou has a documented philatelic history through a set of six carriage labels issued immediately before Guernsey Post assumed responsibility for postal services, making the associated first-day cover also a last-day cover.[3] The issue was described as a statement of island identity, partly because Brecqhou was the only island-wide tenement within Sark’s jurisdiction and one of several islands in the Bailiwick to have issued stamps or carriage labels.[3] The labels used island imagery: the 1d, 2d and 3d values showed a bird in flight, interpreted as a reference to pigeon post, while the 6d and 1 shilling values displayed the Brecqhou shield as an assertion of identity and authority.[3] This local issue fits within the wider postal history of the Bailiwick, where Guernsey developed distinctive stamp issues and obtained postal independence in the late 20th century.[5]

==Public visits==
Public access to Brecqhou is restricted, and a 2010 lighting management plan for Sark stated that visitation to the island is by invitation only.[5] Fort Brecqhou is recorded as a private home, with public access limited to the grounds by appointment and the house itself not open to the public.[4]

==Notes and references==
* Sources on Sark and Brecqhou sometimes use “Sark” in a jurisdictional sense, so references to Sark may include Brecqhou unless the two islands are distinguished explicitly.[2]

* Island-studies scholarship treats Brecqhou’s symbols and postal labels as expressions of separateness from Sark, particularly in relation to Leonard Matchan’s 1960s flag and later stamp issues.[2]

* The island’s small size, proximity to Sark, and difficult landing conditions are commonly cited in discussions of how Brecqhou can be both geographically close to Sark and socially or politically distinct from it.[2]

* Modern academic literature on the Channel Islands includes Brecqhou among the archipelago’s principal islands, while also placing it last in descending order of size.[1]

* Locally produced mapping is important for Brecqhou because the Ordnance Survey does not cover the Channel Islands, and the 2011 States of Guernsey map includes Brecqhou with the Bailiwick’s other mapped islands.[5]

* Scholarship on Sark’s recent political disputes frequently links Brecqhou to the influence of Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and to debates over reform, development, and small-island governance.[4]

* The European Court of Human Rights dispute over Sark’s electoral arrangements is discussed in secondary literature as part of the wider conflict between traditional Sark institutions and the political and economic pressure associated with Brecqhou’s owners.[4]

* Dawes’s article on Brecqhou’s autonomy and related Shima commentary are key secondary sources for modern interpretations of Brecqhou’s constitutional and cultural relationship with Sark.[4]

==Feudal relationship with Sark==
Brecqhou’s constitutional position has been described as part of a “Sark/Brecqhou dyad”, in which jurisdictional contestation has arisen between the island’s owners and the Sark jurisdiction of which it is treated as a part.[1] Sark’s feudal framework originated with the 1565 grant of the Seignory of Sark to Helier de Carteret as a fief haubert, under which the Seigneur owed homage and military service to the monarch in return for extensive rights over land, sea, law and government.[3] The grant required de Carteret to keep Sark defended with forty armed men, and later commentary has argued that this defensive obligation would naturally have extended to Brecqhou because of its proximity to Sark.[4]

Brecqhou has generally been treated as forming part of Sark’s fief, in the same way that Sark’s jurisdiction extends to nearby islets, reefs and rocks.[4] This position has been disputed, notably by Peter Rivett, who argued that Brecqhou was not originally part of the fief and was only later claimed, partly because it was not originally one of the tenements.[4] The modern official position, however, has been that Sark is a separate jurisdiction within the Bailiwick of Guernsey and that it includes Brecqhou.[4]

Because Brecqhou is treated as an island tenement of the fief of Sark, legislation made by Sark’s Chief Pleas applies to it.[4] Some Sark laws have nevertheless been disapplied to Brecqhou, giving it a special status that has been described as a limited and somewhat contradictory form of autonomy within the fief.[4] Sark remained under hereditary feudal government until 2008, when that system was replaced by an elected assembly.[4]

The modern dispute became especially prominent after Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay bought Brecqhou in 1993 and became involved in Sark politics and legal reform.[5] Under Sark’s traditional tax rules, the purchase made them liable for the treizième, a payment of one-thirteenth of the purchase price to the Seigneur, estimated at about £179,000.[5] The Barclays challenged aspects of Sark’s feudal system on human-rights grounds, contributing to the abolition of male-preference inheritance rules and to the 2007 replacement of the treizième with a property transfer tax payable to the island government.[5]

==Fort Brecqhou==
The island’s principal modern residence is a private granite-clad castle commissioned for Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay and designed by the British architect John Quinlan Terry.[2] Construction began in 1994 and was completed in 1996, with the house, gardens and outbuildings reportedly costing about £90 million.[2] Before the development, Brecqhou had one main house; the later works added Fort Brecqhou and several smaller buildings.[3]

The house is built on a quadrangular plan in a castellated Gothic style, with circular turrets at its four corners.[2] Its design includes large twisted chimneys in a Tudor-derived English style, while commentary has described the structure as being built from two types of Spanish granite.[1][2] The DiCamillo Companion and *The Critic* have both described Fort Brecqhou as the largest house built in Britain for at least two centuries.[1][2]

Fort Brecqhou has remained a private home rather than a public visitor attraction, although the grounds have been listed as accessible by appointment.[2] A Sark exterior lighting plan noted security lighting at Brecqhou’s north-coast slipway and lighting for a helicopter landing area, with a 10:30 pm light curfew in place on the island.[4] The building has also been treated in local historical writing as a major feature of the island’s modern period, including in Peter J. Rivett’s history *Brecqhou: A Very Private Island*.[5]
